title: Granary-style malted loaf
subtitle: A British everyday loaf with depth
excerpt: A tin loaf made with malted grain flour and a splash of treacle. The crust stays crisp for about an hour after it comes out of the oven, then softens overnight into the loaf Britain puts in lunchboxes.
sourceNotes: Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136, bread chapter; the enriched tin-loaf formula with treacle and lard dates to mid-Victorian home baking. Malted grain flour came to prominence through Hovis (1887) but the pattern of mixing white and malted flours predates the commercial brand by decades in domestic receipt books.

recipe.freezeNotes: Slice the cooled loaf and freeze flat in a bag. Toast from frozen.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Mix and shape the evening before. Retard the shaped loaf covered in the fridge overnight; bake in the morning, adding 10 minutes.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven; lower to 200°C after 15 minutes
baking.steamMethod: small roasting tin of boiling water on the oven floor for the first 15 minutes

glossaryTerms.definition: Stretch a small piece of dough thin enough to see light through without it tearing. Tells you gluten development is complete.

content.text: Malted grain flour gives a loaf something plain white bread never quite manages: a faint sweetness in the crumb and a complexity in the crust that reads as toasty without being heavy. This is not a loaf that demands much of the baker, but it does ask for a full bulk ferment and a confident bake.

Use 400 g of malted grain flour (the type sold as granary-style or malted wheat) and 100 g of strong white bread flour. The white flour keeps the dough workable; pure malted grain doughs can become dense without it. Active dried yeast or instant yeast both work. If you use active dried, dissolve it in the warm water before mixing.

Combine both flours, yeast, and salt in a large bowl. Add the treacle and softened butter, then pour in most of the water, holding back about 20 ml. Mix until a rough dough forms. Malted grain flour absorbs water slowly; the dough will look shaggy at first. Let it rest 5 minutes before deciding whether to add the remaining water. The finished dough should be soft, slightly tacky, and not pulling away cleanly from the bowl. A 68% hydration dough sticks a little to your hands.
Turn out onto a lightly floured surface and knead for 10 minutes, or 7 minutes in a stand mixer on medium. The dough is ready when it passes the
. Malted grain flour contains the whole kernel, so the dough will always feel slightly rougher than a white-flour dough, even at full development.

Shape the dough into a ball, place in a lightly oiled bowl, cover with a damp cloth or cling film, and leave in a warm spot. The dough should double. A kitchen at 22°C takes about 90 minutes. A cooler kitchen takes longer; a warm one goes faster. Go by the dough, not the clock.

Grease a 900 g / 2 lb loaf tin. Knock back the dough and shape it into a tight log the length of the tin. Place it seam-side down and cover loosely. Proof for about 60 minutes at room temperature, until the dough crowns just above the tin edge. The poke test tells you when: a finger pressed into the side should spring back slowly but not immediately.

Heat the oven to 220°C fan. Place a small roasting tin of boiling water on the oven floor to generate steam. Bake the loaf for 15 minutes, then remove the water tin and drop the temperature to 200°C fan. Continue for another 20 minutes, until the loaf is deep brown and sounds hollow when you knock the base. An instant-read thermometer through the base should read 96°C. Domestic ovens drift; if the crust is colouring fast, drop to 190°C sooner rather than later. Cool on a wire rack for at least an hour before slicing. The crumb needs time to set.

attrs.problem: The loaf didn't rise in the tin

content.text: The yeast may have been added to water too hot (above 40°C kills it) or the bulk ferment didn't run long enough. Proof in a warm place and wait for the dough to clearly double before shaping.
The loaf was sliced too soon. Let it cool completely on a rack, at least 1 hour, before cutting. The steam inside the crumb finishes the bake as the loaf rests.
